name: raylib-rs
description: Rust bindings to raylib, the popular simple C game library; large tutorial ecosystem

kind.comment: Safe Rust bindings over the raylib C game library; Rust is the gameplay language while raylib provides the runtime. Secondary character is framework-like (code-first game loop), but its defining trait is being bindings.

license.comment: Both the raylib-rs binding and the underlying raylib C library are Zlib/libpng licensed.

repository.comment: Current home of the published `raylib` crate. The project originated at deltaphc/raylib-rs; the 6.0 rework and current releases live in the raylib-rs GitHub org.

documentation.comment: API docs on docs.rs; the underlying raylib C API is documented at raylib.com with an extensive cheatsheet and examples.

first-release.comment: First crates.io publish (v0.9.0) on 2019-01-24. The GitHub project (deltaphc/raylib-rs) was created in mid-2018.

latest-stable-version.comment: Published 2026-06-10; a near-complete rework of the binding tracking raylib 6.0. Checked 2026-07-06.

maintenance-status.comment: raylib-rs/raylib-rs last pushed 2026-07-05, with a 6.0.0 release in June 2026. Checked 2026-07-06.

maturity.comment: Wraps the mature raylib C library and has existed since 2019, but the Rust binding API still churns across major versions (6.0 was a near-complete rework), so 'stable' rather than 'mature'.

backing.comment: Community-maintained bindings (raylib-rs org, originally by deltaphc). The underlying raylib C library is led by Ramon Santamaria (raysan5) with community and sponsor support.

implementation.comment: Dual-crate design: raylib-sys (raw FFI) plus a safe `raylib` wrapper. The build script compiles vendored raylib C source via CMake.

architecture.comment: raylib is an immediate-mode library: you run your own game loop and issue draw calls each frame. No ECS, scene graph, or node tree.

built-in-ecs.comment: No ECS; game state is plain Rust structs in a user-driven loop.

dimensionality.comment: Genuine first-party 3D tooling: Camera3D, meshes, models, billboards, heightmaps, plus full 2D drawing.

scripting.comment: Gameplay is written in Rust; no embedded scripting layer.

data-driven-scenes.comment: No scene/prefab format; everything is constructed in code.

reflection-serialization.comment: No first-class runtime reflection or scene serialization; users can add serde themselves.

hot-reload.comment: No built-in code or asset hot-reload.

visual-editor.comment: No scene/level editor. raylib has separate standalone tools (rGuiLayout, rTexPacker, etc.) but they are not part of the Rust bindings.

asset-pipeline.comment: Convenient synchronous loaders for many common formats (textures, fonts/TTF, models/glTF/OBJ, audio WAV/OGG/MP3/FLAC), but no async loading, no processing/reprocessing pipeline, and no hot reload — above manual include_bytes! (1) yet below basic async loading (3).

cli-project-tooling.comment: No dedicated CLI, cargo plugin, or official project template; you use plain cargo.

debug-profiling-tools.comment: Only a DrawFPS helper; no integrated profiler, Tracy, or diagnostics overlay.

ide-support.comment: Nothing beyond standard Rust tooling (rust-analyzer works out of the box).

rendering-backend.comment: raylib renders through its rlgl abstraction over OpenGL (1.1/2.1/3.3/4.3 and ES 2.0). On web it targets HTML5/WebGL via emscripten.

pbr.comment: raylib's material system supports PBR maps and ships a PBR example shader, but PBR is not an automatic turnkey pipeline like Bevy's — it requires wiring up the provided shaders/materials yourself.

lighting.comment: Lighting is forward and driven by user/example shaders (the bundled rlights forward Blinn-Phong helper and PBR example), not an automatic engine lighting system.

shadows.comment: No built-in shadow system; real-time shadow mapping exists only as an example shader you integrate yourself.

custom-shaders.comment: Full GLSL shader support, including model shaders and postprocessing shaders, loadable and slotted into the pipeline.

sprites-tilemaps.comment: First-party 2D sprite drawing (textures and source-rectangle regions, internally batched). No dedicated tilemap abstraction — tilemaps are drawn manually from a texture atlas.

particle-system.comment: No built-in particle/VFX system; particle effects are hand-rolled (example-level).

post-processing.comment: raylib explicitly supports postprocessing shaders applied to a full-screen RenderTexture; effects like bloom/blur are provided as example shaders.

text-rendering.comment: Built-in font loading (including TTF) with glyph-atlas generation and DrawText/DrawTextEx.

physics.comment: No physics engine in the bindings; raylib offers only collision-check helpers (CheckCollision*). raylib's physac is a separate single-header library not exposed by default.

audio.comment: Built-in audio (raylib's raudio, backed by miniaudio): sound and music streaming for WAV, OGG, MP3, FLAC, XM, MOD.

input.comment: First-party keyboard/mouse, gamepad, and touch/gesture input.

animation.comment: Skeletal (bone) animation for 3D models via ModelAnimation (IQM/glTF/M3D); the Rust binding wraps it with a ModelAnimations RAII type. Sprite-frame animation is done manually.

ui-system.comment: raygui, raylib's immediate-mode GUI, is exposed by the bindings (57 functions at 6.0 parity). It is an official raylib companion library rather than a full retained UI framework.

networking.comment: No networking/multiplayer support; bring your own crate.

gltf-model-import.comment: Loads standard 3D model formats including glTF/GLB, OBJ, IQM, and M3D.

linux.comment: Native Linux, with Wayland support available (X11 by default).

web-wasm.comment: Compiles to the wasm32-unknown-emscripten target and runs in-browser via WebGL; the project publishes a browser gallery of ~229 ported examples.

ios.comment: No official iOS support in the Rust bindings (raylib C has some mobile support, but it is not exposed/tested here).

android.comment: Android is explicitly not supported by the Rust bindings.

consoles.comment: No documented console path through the Rust bindings. raylib C has community console ports, but there is no verifiable, reproducible route via raylib-rs.

clean-build-time.comment: Not measured in this environment (no compile performed). Note: the first build compiles vendored raylib C via CMake, adding one-time C-toolchain cost on top of the small Rust surface.

incremental-build-time.comment: Not directly measured. Estimated fast: the Rust dependency tree is minimal with no heavy proc-macro/codegen crates, so once raylib C is compiled, one-line rebuilds are quick.

binary-size.comment: Not directly measured. Estimated small: statically links the compact raylib C library into a minimal Rust binary; typical raylib apps land in the low single-digit-to-low-double-digit MB range.

web-bundle-size.comment: Not measured; emscripten output size for a minimal app was not built in this environment.

runtime-overhead.comment: Not measured. raylib is a lightweight immediate-mode C library, so overhead is expected to be low, but idle CPU/memory was not profiled here; prefer null over a guess.

github-stars.comment: raylib-rs/raylib-rs (current home of the crate) as of 2026-07-06. The original deltaphc/raylib-rs repo, where the project started, carries ~927 stars but no longer publishes the crate.

crates-io-downloads.comment: All-time downloads of the `raylib` crate as of 2026-07-06.

shipped-titles.comment: No widely-documented commercial game shipped specifically with the Rust bindings. The underlying raylib C library has many shipped games, but that is not attributable to raylib-rs.

community-size.comment: The Rust-binding community itself is small (a handful of active contributors). The broader raylib ecosystem community (Discord, forums) is large but centers on the C library.

plugin-asset-ecosystem.comment: No third-party plugin registry for the bindings. raylib bundles official companion libraries (raygui, raymath, rlgl), but there is minimal Rust-side plugin ecosystem.

learning-resources.comment: Very large raylib tutorial/example ecosystem (cheatsheet, ~229 examples ported to Rust, abundant C tutorials and videos that transfer directly), but no dedicated Rust-specific book — hence 4 rather than 5.
